nativeRange.habitat: Coral reef slopes and lagoons in the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden, typically to about 50 m depth. Juveniles tend to congregate in larger groups around the bases of reefs, while adults are commonly solitary or paired on outer reef slopes.

diet.notes: In the wild, the Red Sea Bannerfish feeds on zooplankton and small benthic invertebrates — animals that live on or near the reef floor. In captivity it generally accepts a varied diet of mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood, and marine-algae or spirulina-based preparations such as nori. Frequent small feedings two to three times daily are commonly advised, particularly for newly acquired specimens, which tend to be shy feeders at first.

avoidWith: other Heniochus or similar-patterned butterflyfishes (unless introduced simultaneously)

summary: The Red Sea Bannerfish is a distinctive reef fish from the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden, reaching around 18 cm and recognizable by its long trailing dorsal filament and two broad black bands. Generally considered peaceful, it is best suited to fish-only systems rather than reef tanks, as it tends to nip corals and small invertebrates.
careNotes:
The Red Sea Bannerfish is an active mid-water swimmer that tends to benefit from a long-footprint tank with both ample open swimming space and complex rockwork for refuge. Newly imported specimens are commonly shy and may take time to acclimate; they tend to settle better in calm tanks without aggressive or overly boisterous tankmates. The species can be kept singly or as a pair when both individuals are introduced simultaneously; conspecifics and other Heniochus species are best avoided unless added at the same time, as established adults have been reported to be aggressive toward similar-patterned newcomers. Juveniles enter the trade noticeably smaller than the adult size range.

Reef compatibility: Like most Heniochus, this species feeds on benthic invertebrates and small polyps in the wild and has been reported to nip LPS and SPS corals (hard and stony coral types), zoanthids, tubeworms, ornamental shrimp, and the mantles of tridacnid clams. It is generally not considered reef safe and is best kept in FOWLR (fish-only with live rock) or fish-dominant systems.
breedingNotes: Reported to be oviparous and to form monogamous pairs during the breeding season in the wild. Captive breeding has not been meaningfully documented and is generally considered impractical in home aquariums.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous; forms monogamous pairs during the breeding season in the wild. Captive breeding is not meaningfully documented in home aquariums.
